---
title: "01 · Groups — Symmetry and Structure"
subtitle: "Sets with one operation that satisfies four axioms — the universal language of symmetry"
lesson-id: "t6.groups"
tier: 6
prerequisites: ["t5.sets-logic-proofs", "t1.divisibility-primes"]
unlocks: ["t6.rings-fields", "t6.group-actions"]
applied-domains: ["research", "encryption"]
estimated-minutes: 30
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Have you done [T5 L11 — Sets, Logic, Proof](../../tier-5-undergrad-core/11-sets-logic-proofs/index.qmd)?
2. Comfortable with set notation and basic proof techniques?
3. Recall modular arithmetic ($\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}$) and rotation
   matrices?
:::

## Touch

Pick a system: integers with $+$, hours on a clock, shuffles of three
cards, or invertible matrices. The widget unpacks each one and shows
the four common rules they all obey.

```{ojs}
viewof grpSel = Inputs.select(["(ℤ, +): integers under addition", "(ℤ/5, +): integers mod 5", "Symmetric group S_3: permutations of 3 items", "GL(2, ℝ): invertible 2×2 matrices"], {value: "(ℤ/5, +): integers mod 5", label: "Group:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  let info;
  switch(grpSel) {
    case "(ℤ, +): integers under addition": info = "Closure: a+b ∈ ℤ. Associative. Identity: 0. Inverse of a: −a. Infinite, abelian (a+b = b+a)."; break;
    case "(ℤ/5, +): integers mod 5": info = "Elements {0,1,2,3,4}. 3+4 = 7 ≡ 2 (mod 5). Identity 0. Inverse of 3 is 2. Finite (order 5), abelian, cyclic (generated by 1)."; break;
    case "Symmetric group S_3: permutations of 3 items": info = "6 elements (permutations of {1,2,3}). Composition. Identity = do nothing. Each permutation has an inverse. NON-abelian: order matters."; break;
    case "GL(2, ℝ): invertible 2×2 matrices": info = "Matrix multiplication. Identity I. Inverse exists since det ≠ 0. Infinite, non-abelian. The first place most students meet a non-abelian group with continuous structure."; break;
  }
  return html`<div style="margin: 1rem 0;">
    <div style="font-family:monospace; font-size:1rem; color:#c2185b; text-align:center;">${info}</div>
  </div>`;
}
```

## Wonder

1. Why do four axioms (closure, associativity, identity, inverses)
   capture so much?
2. Some groups are **abelian** ($a \cdot b = b \cdot a$); some aren't.
   Why does this matter?
3. The integers $\mathbb{Z}$ under multiplication: is this a group?
   (Hint: inverse of 3 is 1/3 — not an integer.)

## Discover

### Problem 1

A **group** $(G, \cdot)$ satisfies:

(G1) **Closure:** $a \cdot b \in G$ for all $a, b \in G$.
(G2) **Associativity:** $(a \cdot b) \cdot c = a \cdot (b \cdot c)$.
(G3) **Identity:** there exists $e \in G$ with $e \cdot a = a \cdot e = a$.
(G4) **Inverses:** for each $a$, there exists $a^{-1}$ with $a \cdot
a^{-1} = a^{-1} \cdot a = e$.

A group is **abelian** if also $a \cdot b = b \cdot a$.

Verify $(\mathbb{Z}, +)$ is a group. Identity? Inverse of 5?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Closure ✓. Associative ✓. Identity 0 ($0 + a = a$). Inverse of $a$:
$-a$ (since $a + (-a) = 0$). Inverse of 5: $-5$.

$(\mathbb{Z}, +)$ is an abelian group of infinite order.
</details>

### Problem 2

Show $(\mathbb{Z}, \cdot)$ (integers under multiplication) is **not** a
group.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Identity is 1. Find the inverse of 5.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$5 \cdot ? = 1 \Rightarrow ? = 1/5 \notin \mathbb{Z}$. **No inverse
in $\mathbb{Z}$.** So G4 fails.

$(\mathbb{Q} \setminus \{0\}, \cdot)$ *is* a group: every non-zero
rational has an inverse in $\mathbb{Q}$.
</details>

### Problem 3

The **symmetric group** $S_3$: all permutations of $\{1, 2, 3\}$ under
composition. There are $3! = 6$ elements. Show $S_3$ is non-abelian.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Two permutations: $\sigma = (1 2)$ (swap 1, 2) and $\tau = (2 3)$ (swap
2, 3). Compute $\sigma \tau$ vs $\tau \sigma$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sigma \tau$: first swap 2, 3, then swap 1, 2. $1 \to 1 \to 2$, $2
\to 3 \to 3$, $3 \to 2 \to 1$. Net: $1 \to 2, 2 \to 3, 3 \to 1$ — a
3-cycle.

$\tau \sigma$: first swap 1, 2, then swap 2, 3. $1 \to 2 \to 3$, $2
\to 1 \to 1$, $3 \to 3 \to 2$. Net: $1 \to 3, 2 \to 1, 3 \to 2$ — the
*other* 3-cycle.

Different! So $S_3$ is non-abelian.

The symmetric groups $S_n$ are central: every finite group embeds in
some $S_n$ (Cayley's theorem).
</details>

### Problem 4

The **order** of $G$ is $|G|$ (number of elements). The **order of an
element** $a$ is the smallest positive $n$ with $a^n = e$.

For $\mathbb{Z}/6$ under +, what's the order of element $2$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$2 + 2 = 4, 4 + 2 = 0 \pmod 6$. So $2$ has order $3$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Order $3$.

**Lagrange's theorem:** in a finite group, the order of every element
divides $|G|$. Here $3 | 6$ ✓.

Lagrange constrains the possible structures of finite groups.
</details>

### Problem 5

A **subgroup** $H \le G$ is a subset that is itself a group under the
restricted operation.

Find all subgroups of $\mathbb{Z}/6$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\{0\}$, $\{0, 3\}$ (order 2), $\{0, 2, 4\}$ (order 3), and the
whole group (order 6).

These are *all* divisors of 6 (1, 2, 3, 6). For cyclic groups, the
subgroups correspond exactly to divisors of the order — a complete
classification.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
**Évariste Galois** died in a duel in Paris on 31 May 1832, at age
**twenty**. The night before, he stayed up scribbling letters to
his friend Auguste Chevalier, sketching the group-theoretic ideas
that would explain *which* polynomial equations are solvable by
radicals — the foundations of what we now call **Galois theory**.
The manuscripts sat unread for fourteen years before **Joseph
Liouville** finally published them in 1846. Earlier permutation
work by **Lagrange** (1770) and **Cauchy** (1815) had laid the
ground; Galois saw what it was *for*.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
A group is the algebra of *reversible moves*. The four axioms are
the bare minimum required for "do this, then undo it" to make
sense — and from that single demand, the entire landscape of
symmetry, from clock arithmetic to Rubik's cube to elliptic-curve
cryptography, falls out.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

A **group** $(G, \cdot)$ is a set with operation satisfying:
closure, associativity, identity $e$, inverses $a^{-1}$.

**Abelian:** $a \cdot b = b \cdot a$ for all $a, b$.

**Order of $G$:** $|G|$. **Order of element $a$:** smallest $n > 0$
with $a^n = e$ (or $\infty$).

**Subgroup** $H \le G$: subset that is a group on its own.

**Lagrange's theorem:** in a finite group, $|H|$ divides $|G|$. So
order of every element divides $|G|$.

**Examples:**

- $(\mathbb{Z}, +)$ — infinite abelian.
- $(\mathbb{Z}/n, +)$ — cyclic of order $n$.
- $((\mathbb{Z}/p)^*, \cdot)$ for prime $p$ — cyclic of order $p - 1$
  (used in cryptography).
- $S_n$ — permutations of $n$ items, order $n!$, non-abelian for $n \ge
  3$.
- $D_n$ — dihedral group, symmetries of a regular $n$-gon, order $2n$.
- $GL(n, \mathbb{R})$ — invertible matrices, non-abelian.

A **homomorphism** $\phi: G \to H$ preserves the operation: $\phi(a
\cdot b) = \phi(a) \cdot \phi(b)$. An **isomorphism** is an invertible
homomorphism.

:::

> *Group = closure + associativity + identity + inverses;*
> *Abelian or not — that's the central distinction;*
> *Lagrange constrains finite groups; subgroups divide the order.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Show $\mathbb{Z}/4$ and $\mathbb{Z}/2 \times \mathbb{Z}/2$ are *both*
> groups of order 4 but are not isomorphic.

In $\mathbb{Z}/4$: element $1$ has order $4$ (cyclic). In $\mathbb{Z}/2
\times \mathbb{Z}/2$: every non-identity element has order $2$.

So no isomorphism preserving order can exist.

There are exactly **two groups of order 4** (up to isomorphism):
cyclic $\mathbb{Z}/4$ and Klein four-group $V = \mathbb{Z}/2 \times
\mathbb{Z}/2$.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Identity element of $(\mathbb{Q}, \cdot)$? (Note: this fails
G4 at 0.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$1$. (Need to remove 0 to get a group: $(\mathbb{Q} \setminus \{0\},
\cdot)$.)
</details>

**W2.** Is $(\mathbb{N}, +)$ a group?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**No** — no inverses (no negatives).
</details>

**W3.** Order of $S_4$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$4! = 24$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Is $(\mathbb{R}, \cdot)$ a group?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
No — 0 has no inverse. $(\mathbb{R} \setminus \{0\}, \cdot)$ is.
</details>

**P2.** Find the inverse of $3$ in $\mathbb{Z}/7$ (mult. group of
units).

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$3 \cdot 5 = 15 \equiv 1 \pmod 7$. So $3^{-1} = 5$.
</details>

**P3.** Subgroups of $\mathbb{Z}/12$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Cyclic, so subgroups correspond to divisors of 12: orders 1, 2, 3,
4, 6, 12. **Six subgroups.**
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** **The group of symmetries of a square ($D_4$) has order 8.**
List its elements (rotations and reflections).

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
4 rotations: $r_0$ (identity), $r_{90}, r_{180}, r_{270}$.
4 reflections: across horizontal, vertical, two diagonals.

Total 8. Non-abelian (rotation then reflection ≠ reflection then
rotation).

This is the **dihedral group** $D_4$. Crystals, molecular symmetries,
and Rubik's cube all involve dihedral or symmetric groups. Group
theory captures *all* possible symmetries.
</details>

**S2.** **Groups in cryptography.** RSA uses $(\mathbb{Z}/n)^*$. Diffie-
Hellman uses any cyclic group with hard discrete logarithm. Why does
*group structure* matter for security?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Cryptographic schemes need:** (a) operations that are easy *one
way* but hard to reverse, (b) algebraic structure that supports
encoding and verification.

Cyclic group structure ensures that exponentiation $g^x$ is well-
defined and efficient. The **discrete log problem** ("given $g, g^x$,
find $x$") is hard in well-chosen groups (e.g., $(\mathbb{Z}/p)^*$
for large prime $p$, or *elliptic curve* groups).

Quantum computers break some of these (Shor's algorithm), motivating
*post-quantum* cryptography that uses different group structures
(lattices).

Group theory is the *backbone* of modern cryptography.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"All groups are abelian."** Most are not. $S_n$ for $n \ge 3$,
   $D_n$, $GL(n, \mathbb{R})$ are non-abelian.
2. **"Subgroups have any order."** No — Lagrange forces $|H| \mid
   |G|$.
3. **"Group = set with operation."** Need *all four* axioms.
   $(\mathbb{N}, +)$ has 3 of 4 (no inverses) — not a group.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **A [group](/glossary.qmd#group) is a set with one operation
   plus four axioms** — closure, associativity, identity, inverses
   — and that minimal package captures every notion of *symmetry*.
2. **Most groups are non-[commutative](/glossary.qmd#commutative)**:
   in $S_n$ ($n \ge 3$), in $D_n$, and in invertible matrices,
   $a \cdot b \ne b \cdot a$, and that single fact drives most of
   the subject's depth.
3. **[Lagrange's theorem](/glossary.qmd#lagranges-theorem) constrains
   what subgroups can exist** — the size of any subgroup must divide
   the size of the whole group.

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you state the four group axioms?
2. Without scrolling back, what's the order of $S_5$?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

(Answer: $5! = 120$.)

- **3 yes:** ready for **[02 · Rings & Fields](../02-rings-fields/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo **S2** (groups in cryptography).
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover P3 and P5*.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[T5 L11 — Sets, Logic, Proof](../../tier-5-undergrad-core/11-sets-logic-proofs/index.qmd)**
- **[T1 L15 — Divisibility & Primes](../../tier-1-foundations/15-divisibility-and-primes/index.qmd)**

### What this unlocks

- **[02 · Rings & Fields](../02-rings-fields/index.qmd)** — adds a
  second operation.
- **[03 · Group Actions](../03-group-actions/index.qmd)** — groups
  acting on sets.
- **Tier 7 — Galois Theory, Lie Groups** — deeper abstract algebra.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Encryption.** RSA, Diffie-Hellman, ECDSA — every classical
cryptographic scheme uses group theory.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Physics & chemistry.** Symmetry groups classify molecular
structures, crystal lattices, and elementary particles. The Standard
Model is a gauge theory of certain Lie groups.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Mathematical research.** Groups are the algebraic side of symmetry.
Galois theory (Tier 7) uses groups to determine which polynomial
equations are solvable by radicals.
:::
